title: Types of Electrolyzers
introText: Understanding the different types of electrolyzers is crucial for optimizing hydrogen production.

content.value: Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) water electrolysis is an electrochemical process that splits water into hydrogen and oxygen using electrical energy.

The core of the system is the solid polymer electrolyte membrane. This membrane conducts protons across to the cathode while acting as an electrical insulator and preventing the product gases from mixing.

list.label: Alkaline Electrolysis

content.value: Unlike PEM electrolysis, Alkaline Water Electrolysis operates in a high-pH liquid environment and relies on the movement of hydroxide anions OH-.
The system uses a highly concentrated aqueous alkaline solution, typically 20-30 wt% KOH, in which the electrodes are immersed.

content.alt: Diagram of Alkaline liquid electrolyte system

disadvantages: Gas crossover can occur at low loads

list.label: AEM Electrolysis

content.value: AEM electrolysis essentially bridges the gap between the PEM and traditional alkaline methods, combining the use of a solid polymer membrane with an alkaline operating environment.

content.alt: Diagram of AEM solid membrane system

content.value: The process uses a solid Anion Exchange Membrane (AEM) designed to selectively transport negatively charged hydroxide anions OH- across to the anode, while physically keeping the product gases separated.

advantages: Combines the benefits of a solid membrane and an alkaline environment
Eliminates the need for highly concentrated corrosive liquid electrolytes
disadvantages: Membrane durability is still an industry challenge
Less commercially mature than Alkaline and PEM
